Items.Key: potential resource exhaustion in poolallocator and expressionbuffer management|resource exhaustion / denial of service (dos)|cwe-414 / cwe-401|medium|0,85|enhanced_calc.cpp|665|666|calculator::supercalc::supercalc()|0668: return {};0669: thread_pool_ = std::make_unique<threading::threadpool>(); ...|if the user repeatedly inputs complex expressions and the execution is only scheduled (e.g., if async calculations fail or are ignored without aggressive error handling), expressions or dynamically allocated system resources could eventually exhaust available pooled memory or potentially the heap if manual cleanup fails. while `cleanup()` attempts to free the pool memory based on logic 0884, lack of bounding the number of scheduled analyses might lead to issues.|looping execution of a function that uses expression evaluation (e.g., `run()` loop in `supercalc::run()` method, method `operator*`/`std::stod` in `evaluate_expression` in section 9)|implement limits on registered expressions, track the number of currently scheduled tasks, and consider implementing circuit breaking for thread scheduling if memory usage approaches `config::limits::max_memory_pool_size`.

out-of-bounds access in variable store/variable access|heap-based error / access violation|cwe-125 / cwe-401|low|0,5|enhanced_calc.cpp|287|288|namespace math_engine::variablestore::set_variable|0285: void set_variable(const std::string& name, double value) { 0286: std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(variables_mutex_); 0287: variables_[name] = value; 0288: } |this implementation correctly uses `std::unordered_map::operator[]` within a mutex lock to store data. however, accessing the name via `std::stod` (line 0878) upon an unexpected mathematical expression might lead to exceptions if input parsing is insufficiently robust or if numeric keys overflow potential ranges, leading to instability, although current risk is lower because expression strings are derived from user input.|execution path involving implicit assignment to variable store elements via string manipulation (e.g., line 0738 in `super.handle_variable_command`), or potentially complex user inputs leading to non-numeric items being treated as variable names then being retrieved.|input parsing logic in handling complex sequences needs to validate variable names more rigorously and handle parsing failures gracefully to prevent exceptions from propagating and accessing uninitialized or malformed map entries.
